May also partner with other relevant bodies for the purpose to encourage knowledge sharing and benchmarking; and c. Develop an initial policy for the use and maintenance of the system embedding internal control mechanisms to ensure security in access and limitation of actions personnel allowed for employees, even before it becomes operational.

a. Formally set up the Internal Audit Service / Unit with a municipal government department head, SG-24. The internal auditors should be detached from regular functions or those of routine operating character as mentioned in Administrative Order No. 278 issued by then President Corazon C. Aquino in 1992; and
b. Through the Municipal Human Resource Management Office, evaluate the existing manpower complement to check possible transfer of personnel to form the IAS/IAU, revise staffing patterns or create new internal auditor positions in accordance with CSC Memorandum Circular No. 19 s. 1992 and other relevant issuances.

a. For the Local Chief Executive, to direct the concerned
department/personnel in ensuring full implementation of the one-time
cleansing of PPE account balances, draw necessary accounting entries,
measurements, presentation and disclosure requirements as provided in
the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and ensure
completeness of details in the Report on the Physical Count of Property,
Plant, & Equipment (RPCPPE);

b. For Municipal Engineer to submit Report of Completion and other
documents that would evidence the 100% completion of the project as a
valid basis of the MAO for the reclassification of CIP accounts;

c. Through the GSO, observe the necessary protocols in the disposal of
unserviceable properties;

d. For the Inventory Committee to evaluate recorded items below the
capitalization criteria whether these are semi-expendable items
misclassified as PPE which shall have been expensed upon issuance or
these are items with individual values below threshold but which work
together in the form of a group of network asset;

e. For the City Accountant to improve practices in reporting of accounts by:
⚫ Coordinate with and obtain from the Municipal Engineering Office (MEO)
Report of Completion or any other status report of the projects which
remained outstanding in the books as Construction in Progress (CIP)
account for years and draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) transferring
the CIP accounts to the proper asset accounts and catch-up for
depreciation charges; and
⚫ Taking up the necessary journal entries to correct the balances of the
net book value of identified PPE accounts and Government Equity
accounts including reclassification of the semi-expendable items with
costs not exceeding capitalization threshold upon coordination with the
Inventory Committee.

We recommended that Management:

a) Re-evaluate the projects, programs and activities (PPAs) for which the
funds were originally set out for but remain unimplemented as of date in
congruence with the pressing needs of the community;

b) Identify specific PPAs and integrate in Annual Investment Program (AIP) to
maximize the benefits to be derived therefrom. Revisit the NDRRMC-DBM-
DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and other pertinent guidelines
to encompass the four (4) thematic areas of DRRM such as disaster
prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and
disaster rehabilitation and recovery;

c) Efficiently utilize the fund for the purpose designed and streamline
procurement activities to improve the capacity of the Municipality to
respond in emergencies or disasters. Intensify follow-up mechanisms to
concerned agencies to fully implement the housing units and properly turn-
over to the beneficiaries;

d) DRRMO and the related LGU Officials and employees strictly comply with
the accounting and reporting requirements embodied in R.A. 10121 and
COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular 2014-
002 dated April 15, 2014 to ensure that the financial information is
appropriately taken up in the books with adequate disclosures. The same
reports should have been posted in the LGU’s website and full disclosure
portal of the DILG; and

e) DRRMO provide other related reports that may be requested throughout
the audit process in an expeditious manner.

We recommended that Management:

a. Evaluate the necessary requirements of the PPAs not implemented and
incomplete whether these are feasible and if not, may submit status reports
to the SA providing details and terms for possible return of the remaining
funds to be used for other projects that can help improve the locality;

b. Consider assigning a focal person for each project or for every
implementing office to facilitate the timely implementation of PPAs; and

c. Strengthen internal control mechanisms to improve the efficiency in
implementing the projects and to ensure compliance with the rules and
regulations concerning fund transfers including its liquidation.

We recommended that Management:

a) Expedite the process of complying with the minimum requirements set by
Section 12 of RA 9003 on the creation of the Municipal Solid Waste
Management Board (MSWMB);

b) Intensify its efforts to have the existing Solid Waste Management Plan
(SWMP) approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission
(NSWMC) to be in line with the vision of preserving the natural resources of
the Municipality;

c) Devise local ordinances and strictly enforce implementation of the
provisions of Sections 21 and 22 of RA 9003 on the mandatory segregation
of solid wastes and carry out the following:
❖ Strengthen Information and Education Campaign activities by using the
LGU website or social media to promote awareness and encourage
participation from the community in managing solid wastes;
❖ Establish close coordination with barangay officials to ensure that
efforts to improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay
level; and
❖ Impose fines and penalties for any offense made or in violation of
Section 48 of RA 9003; and

d) Prioritize the closure of the open dump, construct and operate a sanitary
landfill as a final disposal site for solid wastes pursuant to the criteria set in
Section 40 of RA 9003 to ensure protection of public health and the
environment.

We recommended that Management:

a. May partner with relevant bodies such as Department of Information and
Communications Technology (DICT), Department of Interior and Local
Government (DILG), National ICT Confederation of the Philippines (NICP),
Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) and other LGUs for knowledge sharing and
benchmarking; and

b. Consider setting-up an Information Technology Office (ITO) or if not yet
possible, may assign organic personnel in the field of IT to initially review the
current flow of information. A collaboration across departments may also be
performed to grasp their requirements known as systems analysis before
proceeding to system design then development, integration and testing,
implementation and maintenance.

a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to maximize the use of the online banking services where bank statement information and other cast services can be conveniently obtained/availed at any time, thus, will aid the Accountant in the timely bank reconciliation of the books of accounts, and in decision-making activities of the LGU with regard cash management; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions on the reconciliation of accounts as required under Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011 as an internal control measure in ensuring correctness of the account balances carried in the FS.

a. Utilize its existing data-gathering tools to generate gender statistics, age and sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information. Other data-producing LGU departments and national program government agencies operating at the local level shall be tapped by the Municipality Planning and Development Office (MPDO) to provide sex-disaggregated information to complement the GAD Database; b. Make operational and available anytime the GAD Database for easy monitoring of GAD projects; c. Set the GAD agenda or identify priority gender issues and/or specific GAD mandates and targets to be addressed over a one-year or three-year term of the Local Chief Executive (LCE) and ensure provision of adequate resources (including personnel); d. May consider consulting with local stakeholders for better formulation of PPAs to be included in the GPB, and proposal of training programs to toughen GFPS in GAD-related concerns and outcomes; e. Revisit the Philippine Plan for Gender-Responsive Development 1995-2025 which cites programs under different sectors like human development, economic and industrial, infrastructure and technology, and special concerns sector that will help in achieving the Sustainable Development Goals or Agenda 2030; f. Properly monitor implementation of GAD programs/projects/activities of the agency to ensure efficiency in managing its resources and effective observance of its objectives; and g. Adopt the GAD-Plan and Budget Monitoring System developed by the DILG for ease in submission of GAD Plan and Budget, and Accomplishment Report as well as receive timely feedbacks for need of updating reports, as applicable.

We recommended that Management:
a. Review its duly constituted Inventory Committee. Tap more people to do the
physical count and set schedule with the various departments/offices to ensure
that complete physical inventory-taking be made. Assign one person in each
department/office who will maintain the records of all property, plant, and
equipment issued to the concerned officers and employees to ensure timely and
complete Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, & Equipment
(RCPPE);
b. Reconcile annually the RPCPPE with the accounting records and investigate
discrepancies noted for its proper valuation in the FS;
c. Strictly implement the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances, draw
necessary accounting entries, measurements, presentation and disclosure
requirements as provided in the COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020;
d. Through the GSO, observe the necessary protocols in the disposal of
unserviceable properties;
e. Allocate budget for premium insurance and direct the General Services Office to
prepare and accomplish the Property Insurance Form (PIF) for uninsured
properties. Insure against any insurable risk the properties, assets, and interests
with the GIF, as administered by the GSIS the soonest possible time to protect
agency assets against losses due to fire, earthquake and other calamities; and
f. Allocate in the annual budget of the Municipality, the estimated cost for titling of
land and create a committee or team that will focus on expediting titling of all
lots/land acquired and for prospective acquisitions thereof to protect its interests
from possible third-party claims.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to maximize the use of the online banking
services where bank statement information and other services can be
conveniently obtained/availed at any time, thus, will aid the Accountant in the
timely reconciliation of the bank and the books of accounts, and in decision-
making activities of the LGU with regard cash management; and
b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions on the
reconciliation of accounts as required under Section 74 of P.D. No. 1445 and
COA Circular No. 96-011 as an internal control measure in ensuring correctness
of the account balances carried in the FS.

We recommended that Management:
a) Come up with detailed inventory control mechanisms that will capture the proper
receipt, storage, issuance, monitoring/controlling and recording of inventory items;
b) Through the Municipal Accountant, maintain perpetual ledger cards for each
commodity/stock and the General Services Officer (GSO) to maintain stock
cards to account for the receipt and disposition of the same. MAO and GSO
regularly reconcile the balances per ledger card and stock cards;
c) Issue supplies with approved Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) and be consolidated
weekly by the GSO in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to be
submitted to the Municipal Accountant for preparation of the Journal Voucher (JV) to
record the expenditures using appropriate expenditure account; and
d) Establish an Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of inventories and
prepare the Report on Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for submission to the
Municipal Accountant to effect necessary adjustments and the Auditor’s Office not
later than January 31 of each year for perusal.

We recommended that Management:
a) Utilize its existing data-gathering tools to generate gender statistics, age and sex-
disaggregated data and other GAD-related information. Other data-producing
LGU departments and national government agencies operating at the local level
shall be tapped by the Municipality Planning Development Office (MPDO) to
provide sex-disaggregated information to complement the GAD Database;
b) Make operational and available anytime the GAD Database for easy monitoring
of GAD projects. The municipality shall consolidate the GAD-related
information from the barangays for data processing, analysis, updating and
maintenance of the GAD database. The consolidated data shall be submitted to
the Province for updating and maintenance of their respective GAD databases;
c) Set the GAD agenda or identify priority gender issues and/or specific GAD
mandates and targets to be addressed over a one-year or three-year term of the
Local Chief Executive (LCE) and ensure provision of adequate resources
(including personnel);
d) May consider consulting with local stakeholders for better formulation of PPAs
to be included in the GPB, and proposal of training programs to toughen GFPS
in GAD-related concerns and outcomes;
e) Revisit the Philippine Plan for Gender-Responsive Development 1995-2025 which
cites programs under different sectors like human development, economic and
industrial, infrastructure and technology, and special concerns sector that will help in
achieving the Sustainable Development Goals or Agenda 2030 set by the United
Nations General Assembly in 2015;
f) Properly monitor implementation of GAD programs/projects/activities of the
agency to ensure efficiency in managing its resources and effective observance of
its objectives; and
g) Adopt the GAD-Plan and Budget Monitoring System developed by the DILG for
ease in submission of GAD Plan and Budget, and Accomplishment Report as
well as receive timely feedbacks for need of updating reports, as applicable.

We recommended that Management:
a. Thoroughly review all the documents attached and ensure compliance with the
requirements for emergency purchase as embodied in R.A. 11469 of the
Bayanihan to Heal as One Act, GPPB Circular 01-2020 before certifying as to
completeness of the supporting documents to the related Disbursement
Vouchers (DVs);
b. Promptly submit copies of the contract and purchase orders including all its
supporting documents within the prescribed timeline in consonance to COA
Circular 2009-001 for review and evaluation of the subject programs, projects
and activities (PPAs); and
c. Comply with the posting and reporting requirements of the GPPB Circular 01-
2020 and DBM LBC No. 125.

We recommended that Management:
a) Identify specific projects, programs and activities (PPAs) with timelines to be
integrated in Annual Investment Program to maximize the benefits to be derived
therefrom. Revisit the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25,
2013 and other pertinent guidelines to encompass the four (4) thematic areas of
DRRM such as disaster prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness,
disaster response, and disaster rehabilitation and recovery;
b) Efficiently utilize the fund for the purpose designed such as accelerating the
procurement of the needed disaster and rescue equipment to improve the
capacity of the Municipality to respond in emergencies or disasters, obtaining the
necessary documents pertaining to the lot acquired to proceed with land
development, and the acquisition of lots for the eight (8) remaining barangays;
c) Re-evaluate the PPAs for which the funds were originally set out for but remain
unimplemented as of date in congruence with the pressing needs of the
community;
d) DRRMO and the related LGU Officials and employees strictly comply with the
accounting and reporting requirements embodied in R.A. 10121 and COA Circular
2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular 2014-002 dated April 15,
2014 to ensure that the financial information is appropriately taken up in the books
with adequate disclosures. The same reports should have been posted in the LGU’s
website and full disclosure portal of the DILG; and
e) DRRMO provide other related reports that may be requested throughout the
audit process in an expeditious manner.

We recommended that Management:
a) Coordinate with the Local Development Council and Local Finance Committee
and promote/encourage optimum utilization of the 20% Economic Development
Fund by adhering with the DILG-DBM JMC 2017-01;
b) Consider creating a specific committee/team that will spearhead the planning
and evaluation of PPAs’ feasibility. Devise a suitable “Project Identification
Process” as course or roadmap in utilizing the fund. Factors such as priority
needs, location, resources available (duration and manpower) be taken into
account;
c) Streamline all other procedures necessary to be undertaken for the PPAs to be
materialized and evaluated;
d) Evaluate the sufficiency and capacity of employed engineers and support staff to
strictly supervise the implementation of PPAs. Actual inspection and report
formulation impact timely evaluation and impose commitment from contracting
parties. Check on applying “geo-tagging” for real-time monitoring and easy access or
update of project accomplishments;
e) Require the MPMC to aptly conduct periodic monitoring and evaluation of the
municipality’s PPA implementation with appropriate reports submitted to the
Mayor for perusal and appropriate action; and
f) Furnish the Auditor’s Office of the required reports for timely review and
validation of accomplishments.

We recommended that Management:
a. Comply with the minimum requirements set by Section 12 of RA 9003 on the
creation of Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB);
b. Intensify its efforts to have the Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) for 2017-
2027 approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission
(NSWMC) to be in line with the vision of preserving the natural resources of the
Municipality;
c. Devise local ordinances and strictly enforce implementation of the provisions of
Sections 21 and 22 of RA 9003 on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes and
carry out the following:
- Strengthen Information and Education Campaign activities by using the LGU
website or social media to promote awareness and encourage participation from
the community in managing solid wastes;
- Establish close coordination with barangay officials to ensure that efforts to
improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay level; and
- Impose fines and penalties for any offense made or in violation of Section 48 of
RA 9003; and
d. Prioritize the closure of the open dump, construct and operate a sanitary landfill as a
final disposal site for solid wastes pursuant to the criteria set in Section 40 of RA
9003 to ensure protection of public health and the environment.

We recommended that Management:
a. Provide adequate support and funding for the construction of necessary facilities and
speed up the provision of water supply;
b. Partner with National Meat Inspection Commission (NMIC) being the agency
assigned for the training and accreditation of personnel on slaughterhouse
operation; and
c. Shall formulate, adopt and implement specific rules and regulations to ensure
efficiency, effectiveness, order and viable operations of the slaughterhouse to
serve as additional source of income that will help in the LGU in providing other
services to the public;

We recommended that Management:
a) Consider setting-up an Information Technology Office (ITO) or if not yet possible,
may assign organic personnel who is equipped for initially reviewing as to how the
system be designed, programmed and implemented in the LGU by phase;
b) May also partner with other relevant bodies for the purpose to encourage knowledge
sharing and benchmarking; and
c) Develop an initial policy for the use and maintenance of the system embedding
internal control mechanisms to ensure security in access and limitation of actions
allowed for employees, even before it becomes operational.

We recommended for Management to allocate budget for premium insurance and require the GSO to insure all insurable properties of the LGU in compliance with RA 656 and Proclamation No. 1081 and General Order No. 1 dated September 22, 1992, to protect agency assets against losses due to fire, earthquake and other calamities.
observations.managementAction: CY 2018 AAR: Compliance with RA 656 and Proclamation No. 1081 and General Order No. 1 dated September 22, 1992, respectively. Upon availability of funds, will allocate the corresponding budget payment.
observations.reason: Due to budgetary constraints.

observations.recommendation: a. Formally set up the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (LDRRMO) as required under NDRRMC/DILG/DBM/CSC JMC No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014, observe guidelines in terms of organization and composition of the LDRRMO, position titles, qualification standards, and salary grades for the technical staff; b. Formulate a comprehensive and integrated LDRRMP in accordance with RA No. 10121 in close coordination with the local development council; c. Prepare a LDRRMFIP with details of funded projects and activities for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness; include unexpended balances of prior years in the plan and investment program.
observations.managementAction: CY 2018 AAR: The LGU is in the process of recruiting regular employee to formally set up a Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management office as required. Risk assessment of all 38 barangays to be conducted. 2020 MDRRMO AIP is now on track. Delay due to late budget approval. Unutilized 2019 Budget will be utilized in 2020. The MDRRM Office will comply with accounting and reporting requirements; posting of reports on LGU website will be observed; documentation and coordination with national agencies are ongoing.
observations.reason: Non-conformity with the structures/staffing patterns/standards prescribed under JMC No. 2014-1; lack of manpower and various intervening activities delayed actions.

observations.recommendation: Include in the annual budget of the Municipality the estimated cost for titling of land and instruct the Municipal Assessor to spearhead the titling of land to facilitate land titling.
observations.managementAction: Directed the Municipal Assessor’s Office to facilitate the land titling and to include the cost of titling in the 2020 Annual Budget. Will allocate funds first quarter of 2020.
observations.reason: Non-inclusion in the 2019 Budget but funds will be allocated first quarter of 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

a. Through the Office of the Municipal Accountant, estimate the recoverable
amount of the totally and partially damaged PPE and draw a journal entry
voucher to set up allowance for impairment of PPE in accordance with
IPSAS 21;
b. Establish a system to ensure proper computation of depreciation of all
PPEs of the agency in accordance with the provisions of PPSAS 17 to
guarantee the correctness of the amount of depreciation of assets reported
under Property, Plant and Equipment and make the necessary adjustments
for the computed depreciation pertaining thereto;
c. Allocate budget for premium insurance and direct the General Services
Office to prepare and accomplish the Property Insurance Form (PIF) for
uninsured properties. Insure against any insurable risk the properties, assets,
and interests with the GIF, as administered by the GSIS the soonest
possible time to protect agency assets against losses due to fire, earthquake
and other calamities;
d. Allocate in the annual budget of the Municipality, the estimated cost for titling of
land and create a committee or team that will focus on expediting titling of all
lots/land acquired and for prospective acquisitions thereof to protect its interests
from possible third-party claims;
e. Review its duly constituted Inventory Committee. Tap more people to do the
physical count and set schedule with the various departments/offices to ensure
that complete physical inventory-taking be made. Assign one person in each
department/office who will maintain the records of all property, plant, and
equipment issued to the concerned officers and employees to help facilitate the
reconciliation of accounts with the General Services Office and Accounting
Office. Also, observe prompt submission of the Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE); and
f. Through the Office of the Municipal Accountant, draw a journal entry
voucher to effect the necessary adjustments to correct the misclassified PPE
accounts.

We recommended that Management:

a. Through the Office of the Municipal Accountant, compute the related
depreciation in consonance with PPSAS 17 and adjust the affected accounts
that are determined to be understated or overstated to present the correct
balances and the necessary disclosures in the financial statements;
b. Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and GSO
the necessary information for cost segregation of road projects; and
c. Prepare the necessary documents and submit the required reports to the
Auditor’s Office not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant facilitate the
submission of request for authority to write-off of dormant accounts supported
with documentation of the actions taken, duly reviewed and approved by the
Head of the Agency.

We recommended that Management:

a. Expedite the implementation of these projects to benefit the intended
beneficiaries;
b. Return the unexpended funds, especially for the completed projects to the
Source Agencies (SA) with appropriate supporting schedules attached;
c. Follow up the remaining 10% from the SA for the release of such funds so as
to pay in full the remaining balance owed to the contractors;
d. Close the completed projects still under CIP account to appropriate
Infrastructure Project account/s and subsequently transfer the said asset
accounts to the General Fund, these being used in the LGU operations and be
provided with appropriate depreciation charges; and
e. For subsequent fund transfers, timely monitor the implementation of PPAs to
realize program objectives beneficial to the community and avoid dormant
amounts in the LGU financial statements.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the concerned personnel to exert extra effort in preparing and
submitting the reports and related supporting documents timely. These
should form part of their performance evaluation standards as various
activities depend on the review of these accounts; and
b. Unjustified failure of officials and employees to submit the required reports
shall be considered ground for automatic suspension of payment of their
salaries until they have complied with the requirements set forth by the law
and other relevant rules and regulations.

We recommended that Management:

a. Thoroughly review the required attachments embodied in R.A. 9184 and
COA Circular 2009-001. A transmittal letter detailing the list of documents
shall be provided and the same be index-tabbed for easy-reference;
b. Submit the documents in its original form. Two sets are required for those
contracts above threshold - ₱2M for goods and services; and ₱5M for
infrastructure projects as these are subject to review and inspection of COA
Regional Office XII Technical Services Office (TSO);
c. Observe the timeline for submission of contracts, POs and supporting
documents for review, evaluation and inspection of projects, programs and
activities (PPAs) implemented by the Agency; and
d. Direct the BAC to prepare the PMR and submit the same to the GPPB in
printed and electronic format per semester.

We recommended that Management:

a. Formally set up the Internal Audit Service / Unit with a municipal
government department head, SG-24. The internal auditors should be
detached from regular functions or those of routine operating character as
mentioned in Administrative Order No. 278 issued by then President
Corazon C. Aquino in 1992; and
b. Through the Municipal Human Resource Management Office, evaluate the
existing manpower complement to check possible transfer of personnel to
form the IAS/IAU, revise staffing patterns or create new internal auditor
positions in accordance with CSC Memorandum Circular No. 19 s. 1992
and other relevant issuances.

We recommended that Management:

a. Formally set-up a Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office as
required under NDRRMC/DILG/DBM/CSC Joint Memorandum Circular
(JMC) No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014. Observe the guidelines under the
JMC in terms of organization and composition of the LDRRMO, position
titles, qualification standards, and salary grades for the technical staff of the
LDRRMO to ensure that its functions, duties and responsibilities are fully
carried out;
b. Consider employing various risk assessment techniques to evaluate the
needs of the communities in the Municipality for formulation of a well-
defined Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Plan and
Fund Investment Plan. Identify specific projects, programs and activities
(PPAs) with timelines to be integrated in Annual Investment Program to
maximize the benefits to be derived therefrom. Revisit the NDRRMC-DBM-
DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and other pertinent guidelines
to encompass the four (4) thematic areas of DRRM such as disaster
prevention and mitigation, disaster preparedness, disaster response, and
disaster rehabilitation and recovery;
c. Efficiently utilize the fund for the purpose designed and re-evaluate the
PPAs for which the funds were originally set out for but remain
unimplemented as of date;
d. DRRMO and the related LGU Officials and employees must strictly comply with
the accounting and reporting requirements embodied in R.A. 10121 and COA
Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and COA Circular 2014-002 dated
April 15, 2014 to ensure that the financial information is appropriately taken up in
the books with adequate disclosures. The same reports should have been posted in
the LGU’s website and full disclosure portal of the DILG;
e. Consider developing a Handbook in handling disaster related activities or
the Comprehensive Public Safety Manual to provide an analysis on the
vulnerabilities of the Municipality and the necessary measures to capacitate
the constituents in minimizing the risks involved. Protocols and coordination
mechanisms among stakeholders may warrant effective and efficient provision of
necessary goods and services;
f. Document accumulated experiences of communities from previous disasters
together with inputs from concerned agencies like MGB and PHIVOLCS to
help in updating the local DRRM Plan and contingency plan to achieve a
sustainable disaster management system;
g. Consider creating a Public Information Services to focus on information
dissemination to the community level through media and barangay
awareness campaign programs, production of reference materials and
coordination with different agencies and sectors to increase understanding
and application of risk reduction measures; and
h. Recommend strategies and programs for appropriation for climate change
adaptation and mitigation. Advocacy for the implementation of the building
code and use of green technology, conduct of inventory, vulnerability and
risk assessments for critical facilities and infrastructure, drills & trainings
are to be included for building a safe, disaster-resilient and adaptive
community.

We recommended that Management:

a. Coordinate with the Local Development Council and Local Finance
Committee and promote/encourage optimum utilization of the 20%
Economic Development Fund by adhering with the DILG-DBM JMC 2017-
01 with emphasis that projects shall be investment or capital expenditures to
ensure sustainability and impact to the lives of the constituents;
b. Specify the implementation target period of the projects in the Annual
Investment Program to serve as a guide in having the documents prepared
for procurement and easier assessment of actual performance against
standards on a periodic basis;
c. Streamline all other procedures necessary to be undertaken for the PPAs to
be materialized and evaluated;
d. Evaluate the sufficiency and capacity of employed engineers and support
staff to strictly supervise the implementation of PPAs. Actual inspection and
report formulation impact timely evaluation and impose commitment from
contracting parties. Check on applying “geo-tagging” for real-time
monitoring and easy access or update of project accomplishments;
e. Require the MPMC to aptly conduct periodic monitoring and evaluation of
the municipality’s PPA implementation with appropriate reports submitted
to the Mayor for perusal and appropriate action; and
f. Furnish the Auditor’s Office of the required reports for timely review and
validation of accomplishments.

We recommended that Management:

a. Strictly enforce implementation of the provisions of Sections 21 and 22 of RA
9003 on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes and carry out the
following:
• Strengthen Information and Education Campaign (IEC) activities by using
the LGU website or social media to promote awareness and encourage
participation from the community in managing solid wastes;
• Establish close coordination with barangay officials to ensure that efforts to
improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay level; and
• Impose fines and penalties for any offense made or in violation of Section
48 of RA 9003.
b. Prioritize the closure of the open dump, construct and operate a sanitary
landfill as a final disposal site for solid wastes to ensure protection of public
health and the environment; and
c. Utilize the available Materials Recovery Facility in Brgy. Leboce which
remain idle to date. Further, provide adequate funding for the construction of a
functional MRF and utilize remaining funds for other SWM programs/activities

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Treasurer in establishing the accurate amount of receivables from each taxpayer and reconcile the discrepancies on the affected receivable accounts to come up with the corrected receivable balances.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation:
We recommended for the inventory committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties and reconcile immediately the results of the count with the property and accounting records to ensure correctness and accuracy of the recorded/reported PPE in the FS.

Recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action to management:
Notify the AOs concerned for the immediate liquidation of all outstanding cash advances pursuant to COA Circulars 97-002 and 2012-004.
Impose sanctions prescribed in the said Circulars for non-compliance thereof, such as withholding the salaries and other emoluments of the officials/employees who continuously disregard, fail or decline to comply with the demand, including filing appropriate charges against them, if warranted.
Ensure strict compliance with COA Circulars 97-002 and 2012-004 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, particularly the period within which to liquidate the same to avoid accumulation of unsettled cash advances.

Recommendation:
In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following course of action:
Prepare promptly all necessary adjusting and correcting entries to record all valid reconciling items reflected in the BRS.
Communicate/coordinate promptly with depository banks to rectify errors in the bank statements.
The municipal treasurer shall send written notices to the payees of the existence of the check at least one month before it becomes stale. As to the stale checks, Municipal Accountant shall prepare an adjusting entry to restore/revert the same to the Cash in Bank and set up an Accounts Payable for each payee.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management the following courses of action:
Send demand letters to NGOs/POs concerned for immediate liquidation of the outstanding unliquidated amount of fund transfer.
Require from the NGOs/POs concerned to submit fund utilization report and certification from the recipient that the project was partially or fully implemented, supported by pictures of the implemented projects.
Submit the copies of approved Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) between the LGU and NGOs/POs concerned to the Office of the Auditor to support in the audit of the said transactions.

Recommendation:
We recommended for management to impose all possible sanctions or penalties and other legal remedies stipulated under the procurement law, such as but not limited to the following:
Collection of liquidated damages in the amount equal to one-tenth of one percent (.01%) of the cost of the unperformed portion for every delay;
Termination of contract based on the grounds and following the procedures stated in the Guidelines on Termination of Contracts; and
Issuance of blacklisting order and forfeiture of performance security in accordance with the provisions of the Uniform Guidelines on Blacklisting.

Recommendation:
We recommended to the management to submit all lacking documents as enumerated above and the missing certificate of turn-over and acceptance of one (1) housing unit in Purok 6, Barangay Batasan.
